title: Draining a Node for Maintenance
description:
## Draining a Node for Maintenance

`kubectl drain` safely evicts all pods from a node before maintenance. It cordons the node (marks it unschedulable), then gracefully evicts every pod — respecting PodDisruptionBudgets and waiting for replacements to schedule elsewhere.

Key flags:
- `--ignore-daemonsets` — required because DaemonSet pods cannot be evicted (they are node-bound)
- `--delete-emptydir-data` — required to evict pods using `emptyDir` volumes (data will be lost)
- `--force` — evict pods not managed by a controller (bare pods)

**Your task:**

The cluster node needs to go into a maintenance window.

1. Find the node name using `kubectl get nodes`
2. **Drain** the node using all three flags above
3. Verify the node STATUS shows `Ready,SchedulingDisabled`

> 💡 In production you would run `kubectl uncordon <node>` after maintenance is complete. The environment will be restored automatically after this scenario.

```bash
NODE=$(kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
kubectl drain $NODE --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --force
```

hints.title: Get the node name and drain it
hints.body: Store the node name in a variable, then run drain with the required flags.
hints.command: NODE=$(kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') && kubectl drain $NODE --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --force

hints.title: Verify the node is cordoned
hints.body: After draining, kubectl get nodes should show Ready,SchedulingDisabled in the STATUS column.

teardown_commands.command: kubectl uncordon $(kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') 2>/dev/null || true
